title: Timeout Handling
concept: Setting strict execution timeouts on tools (like shell commands or database queries) to prevent infinite hangs.

exercises.prompt: Last lesson: when a tool finishes running, what does the agent capture to build a clean observation?

options.text: Just the color of the terminal window
The output text, any error text, and a success/failure code
Only the exact time the tool started

exercises.explain: A clean observation is built from stdout (normal output), stderr (error text), and the return code that says whether it worked. That way the agent knows exactly what happened.

cards.title: The command that never comes back
cards.body: An agent runs a shell command to fetch data. But the server is stuck, so the command just... waits. Forever. The whole agent freezes with it.

cards.title: What is a timeout?
cards.body: A timeout is a strict time limit you set on a tool. If the tool doesn't finish in that many seconds, it gets stopped automatically so the agent can move on.

cards.title: Like a kitchen timer
cards.body: You put noodles on and set a timer for 5 minutes. If something goes wrong and they aren't done, the timer still rings and you check instead of standing there all night.

cards.title: Timeout in action
cards.body: A database query is given a 10-second timeout. The database hangs, so at 10 seconds the query is cancelled. The agent gets a 'timed out' message and tries a smaller query instead.

cards.title: Myth: longer is always safer
cards.body: Some think a huge timeout prevents problems. But a 10-minute limit still lets a stuck tool freeze the agent for 10 minutes. Short, sensible limits keep the agent responsive.

cards.title: Timeout vs. no timeout
cards.body: The difference is whether a stuck tool can ever stop.

left.text: Tool hangs and the agent waits forever, doing nothing.

right.text: Tool is cut off after a set time, and the agent recovers.

exercises.prompt: Why do we set a strict timeout on a tool like a shell command?

options.text: To make the command run faster than normal
To stop the tool if it hangs, so the agent doesn't freeze forever
To hide any error messages from the user
To make the output look more colorful

exercises.explain: A timeout is a safety limit. If a command gets stuck, the timeout cancels it so the agent can report the problem and keep going instead of hanging.

exercises.prompt: True or False: A timeout guarantees a tool will always finish successfully.
exercises.explain: False. A timeout only guarantees the tool will STOP after a set time. It may stop by finishing, or it may be cancelled for taking too long. It doesn't force success.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank about why short limits help.
exercises.sentence: A very long timeout still lets a stuck tool freeze the agent, so a short sensible limit keeps it more responsive.
exercises.explain: Short, sensible limits mean a stuck tool gets cut off quickly, so the agent stays responsive and can recover fast.

exercises.prompt: Which timeout message was written by an AI agent's tool handler?

options.text: Query cancelled: exceeded 10s timeout. Retrying with a narrower filter.
ummm the thing broke i think just try again lol
The database is my friend and we are both very tired today

exercises.explain: The AI handler gives a clear, structured message: what happened (cancelled), why (timeout exceeded), and the next step (retry narrower). The others are casual or nonsense.

exercises.prompt: An agent runs a shell command that hangs. It has a 15-second timeout. What is the correct sequence of events?

options.text: The agent waits forever because the command never returns
At 15 seconds the command is cancelled, a 'timed out' observation is captured, and the agent decides what to do next
The command is ignored immediately and no observation is made
The timeout makes the command finish instantly with a success code

exercises.explain: A timeout sets a hard limit: at 15 seconds the stuck command is stopped, and just like capturing normal outputs, the agent records a clean 'timed out' observation so it can recover and choose the next action. Timeouts turn silent hangs into visible, handleable events.
